name: feat-ESE-0012-02
title: GpuContext: WebGPU device init and buffer pool
description:
Implement GpuContext: the WebGPU device/queue lifecycle manager and GPU buffer pool. Handles lazy device initialization, maps ECS component storages to GPUBuffers, tracks dirty state per component, and manages buffer capacity growth when component storages grow.

Inspired by TypeGPU's lazy resolution pattern and bevy_hanabi's slab allocator.
acceptanceCriteria:
- GpuContext initializes WebGPU device and queue (with graceful fallback error if WebGPU unavailable)
- Buffer pool creates/caches GPUBuffers keyed by component ID + field name
- Buffers auto-grow when component storage grows (ensureCapacity)
- Dirty tracking: cpuDirty set marks components modified on CPU since last upload
- destroy() releases all GPU resources
- Unit tests cover: init, buffer creation, capacity growth, dirty tracking, destroy cleanup
demoDeliverable: Console demo: create a GpuContext, register 3 components (Transform, Velocity, ParticleLife), show buffer pool state. Add 1000 entities, show buffers auto-grew. Mark Transform dirty, show dirty set. Destroy context, show cleanup. All output via console.log with clear labels.
testingScenarios:
- Init succeeds on WebGPU-capable browser, returns error on non-WebGPU
- Buffer created for each component field (px, py, pz each get their own GPUBuffer)
- Buffer grows when component storage doubles capacity
- Dirty tracking correctly reflects CPU writes
- Double-init is idempotent
- Destroy releases buffers and device
stakeholderUnderstanding: GpuContext is the foundation layer — it owns the WebGPU device and maps our existing SoA TypedArrays to GPU storage buffers. Lazy init means we only pay for GPU resources when a gpuSystem is actually registered. Dirty tracking ensures we don't re-upload unchanged data. This must work before any kernel can dispatch.
